hypotheses.title: The €300M base counts the non-intelligent majority of a market the venture cannot sell into
hypotheses.narrative: The venture sells intelligent rack PDUs, but tamBase anchors on Grand View's $2.81B 'rack PDU' figure — and Grand View itself states the NON-intelligent segment held the largest revenue share in 2025. IndexBox confirms the split is real by selling 'Rack PDUs' and 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' as separate EU reports. The scope-down chain carefully audited geography and FX but never audited product type: roughly half the €300M base is basic/metered units the product line does not address. TAM, SAM and YAM all inherit a ~2x product-scope inflation while the ledger label ('rack-PDU market, all segments/buyers') reads as if it were scope-matched.

mechanism.text: Grand View's own segmentation of the $2.81B anchor reports the non-intelligent rack-PDU segment held the largest 2025 revenue share, while the venture's product is intelligent PDUs only

Independent sizings of the intelligent-only subset run $2.1B (GIA 2024) to $3.0B (MRF 2025) against $1.7–3.9B for total rack-PDU — and IndexBox maintains distinct EU reports for 'Rack PDUs' vs 'Intelligent Rack PDUs', confirming the vendor community treats them as different products with different bases

The €300M CE base carries the full-category value; the venture-relevant intelligent-only base is materially smaller, and every downstream funnel stage (SAM €165M, YAM €1.65M) inherits the inflation because serviceableFactor haircuts reach, not product scope

hypotheses.whyMissable: The label 'rack-PDU market' looks perfectly scope-matched to a rack-PDU vendor — the refinement cycle even proved its diligence by removing the PDUs+PSUs ceiling, creating the impression definitional contamination was handled. The remaining contamination sits inside the anchor's own segmentation table, one level below the headline number anyone reads.
hypotheses.falsifier: A scope-pure European source (IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs headline, or an Omdia segment table) showing intelligent/monitored/switched PDUs at ≥75% of European rack-PDU revenue — i.e., Europe's revenue mix is intelligent-dominated even if global unit mix is not.

likelihood.rationale: The anchor source directly states non-intelligent held the largest revenue share globally; the offset is that intelligent units carry 3–5x ASPs and European colo/hyperscale buyers skew intelligent, so the revenue-weighted European intelligent share plausibly lands at 45–60% rather than below 40%

perturbation.note: Restrict the base to the intelligent-PDU-relevant revenue share of the rack-PDU category (~55% revenue-weighted in Europe)

indicators.signal: Intelligent vs non-intelligent revenue split in the next Grand View / Mordor rack-PDU report vintage
indicators.where: Grand View Research rack-PDU report segment tables; Mordor Intelligence Intelligent PDU report

indicators.signal: ASP spread between basic/metered and switched/outlet-metered SKUs on incumbent price lists
indicators.where: Vertiv Geist, Raritan/Legrand, and APC published EU price lists
indicators.threshold: Basic-unit volumes dominating distributor stock at <€500 ASP while intelligent SKUs sit >€1,500

mitigations.action: Commission the IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs report and difference it against the general EU Rack PDUs report to isolate the venture-relevant base
mitigations.note: Two $4,000 reports collapse the product-scope ambiguity that the ±40% band silently absorbs

mitigations.action: Add a metered-only downmarket SKU to the roadmap so the non-intelligent half of the base is addressable rather than excluded
mitigations.note: Converts a TAM-definition problem into a product-line decision

hypotheses.title: The 'Europe ≈ 30%' hop is an inserted number; two sourced regional shares imply Europe is ≤24%
hypotheses.narrative: The single most load-bearing hop in tamBase — Europe's share of the global rack-PDU market — is stated by no source. Grand View publishes only NA = 38%; MarketIntelo puts Asia-Pacific at 38.2% and calls it the DOMINANT region. If both regional leaders hold ~38%, Europe plus rest-of-world share a ~24% residual, and Europe alone plausibly sits at 20–23%, not 30%. The 30% was inferred from NA's share alone, before the APAC datapoint existed in the corpus. A 7-point regional-share error moves the base ~23% by itself, and unlike the tornado's band it is a directional bias, not symmetric noise.

mechanism.text: No retrieved source states a Europe share of the global rack-PDU market; the chain inserts ≈30% by analogy to Grand View's NA = 38%

MarketIntelo independently reports Asia-Pacific dominated the rack-PDU market with 38.2% revenue share; NA 38% + APAC 38.2% leaves ≤23.8% for Europe, MEA and LatAm combined, capping Europe well below the inserted 30%
tamBase is linear in the Europe-share hop: 30%→23% cuts the CE base ~23%, compounding with (not covered by) the ±20% band, and dragging SAM and YAM down proportionally

hypotheses.whyMissable: The expression reads like sourced arithmetic — '$2.81B × Europe ≈ 30% [est.; NA = 38% per same source]' — so a reviewer sees a citation next to the hop and assumes the 30% is anchored. The bracketed 'est.' is the only tell that the decisive multiplier is invented, and the corroborating APAC datapoint lives in a different publisher's report the model never joined against.
hypotheses.falsifier: A published regional revenue table for the rack-PDU category (Grand View regional chapter, IndexBox world-vs-EU headline pair, or Omdia) showing Europe at ≥28% of global revenue.

likelihood.rationale: Two independent sources sum NA+APAC to ~76%, directly constraining Europe below 30%; the counterweight is that MarketIntelo's category scope may differ from Grand View's, so the two shares may not be strictly additive

perturbation.note: Europe share corrected from 30% to ~23% residual; all other hops held constant

indicators.signal: Europe regional revenue share in any rack-PDU-specific report vintage
indicators.where: Grand View rack-PDU report regional segmentation; IndexBox world benchmark report vs EU report headline ratio

indicators.signal: EMEA vs Americas vs APAC rack-power revenue mix in incumbent disclosures
indicators.where: Vertiv 10-K segment reporting; Legrand annual report data-center line commentary
indicators.threshold: EMEA rack-power consistently <25% of global segment revenue

mitigations.action: Purchase the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report and the world benchmark report together to compute Europe's share directly instead of inferring it
mitigations.note: This is the exact hop the ledger's own promotionPath targets; the affordance table already prices the information at €30M of TAM swing

mitigations.action: Rebuild tamBase bottom-up (CE MW × €-per-MW PDU intensity from vendor fit-out BOMs) so the regional-share hop is bypassed entirely
mitigations.note: A second independent method converts the untestable 30% into a cross-checkable intensity assumption

hypotheses.title: Colo-only apportionment plants the flag in Germany/NL while 35–40% of PDU demand sits in non-colo load with a different country mix
hypotheses.narrative: Every geo share divides colo MW by colo MW, but rack-PDU demand tracks TOTAL installed IT load — and the non-colo remainder is not distributed like colocation. Germany's installed load (BMWK >2,700MW) exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW, Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-basis share 0.13 vs 0.08), and Poland's better-corroborated installed-load figure yields 0.10 vs 0.07. EUDCA confirms the gap is continental: colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power. A venture that builds its Year-1 named-account plan on the colo-derived map will staff Frankfurt and Amsterdam while a third of the actual PDU spend — enterprise and self-operated halls in CH, PL, AT — never appears on its target list.

mechanism.text: EUDCA: commercial colo+hyperscale provides just over two-thirds of Europe's IT power — so roughly a third of PDU-consuming load is outside the colo scope every geo.* ratio is built on

The colo-vs-installed divergence is country-specific, not uniform: DE's installed-load method gives 0.42 vs colo 0.50, CH's self-operated stock pushes it to ~0.13 vs 0.08, and PL's triangulated 660MW installed load implies ~0.10 vs 0.07 — the €300M gets reallocated across countries, not just rescaled

The Year-1 plan sells through large operators (colo-centric by construction), so the misallocated map inflates the reachable share of DE/NL and hides demand in CH/PL enterprise halls; the funnel's geography mix shifts against the venture's channel build
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

hypotheses.whyMissable: Each geo node already discloses its colo-vs-installed crossCheck divergence, so the issue looks acknowledged — but it is acknowledged as per-country BAND WIDTH, not as a correlated, same-direction reallocation across all countries at once. Bands on independent nodes cannot express 'the whole dimension is measured in the wrong denominator'.
hypotheses.falsifier: A single-scope, single-source table of total installed IT load for all CE-7 countries (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) whose per-country shares match the current colo-based shares within ±3 percentage points.

likelihood.rationale: The scope gap is documented in the ledger's own crossChecks for four of seven countries and corroborated continentally by EUDCA; the uncertainty is whether non-colo PDU spend per MW is comparable to colo (enterprise refresh cycles are slower, partially offsetting the reallocation)

perturbation.note: Installed-load method share; mix shift, deliberately not renormalized
NL colo-heavy market loses relative weight under installed-load scope
Self-operated Swiss stock enters the denominator

indicators.signal: Publication of country-level installed IT power (not colo-only) for CE-7
indicators.where: EUDCA 'State of European Data Centres' country profiles; BMWK/Bitkom German landscape updates
indicators.threshold: Any CE country's installed-load share diverging >5pp from its colo share

indicators.signal: Origin of inbound PDU demand by facility type in early pipeline
indicators.where: Venture's own CRM plus PMR Poland commercial-DC series and CBRE Suisse commercial-vs-self-operated splits
indicators.threshold: >25% of qualified CE demand coming from enterprise/self-operated sites the colo map does not list

mitigations.action: Reconcile all seven countries onto one installed-load scope before locking Year-1 territory and account assignments
mitigations.note: The DE node carries the largest band swing (−€24M TAM at the low edge) and is the cheapest to resolve — two public sources already bracket it

mitigations.action: Add an enterprise/self-operated sales motion (TÜV-certified retrofit bundle) for CH and PL alongside the large-operator channel
mitigations.note: Hedges the plan against the denominator being wrong without waiting for the data

hypotheses.title: tamBase apportions revenue by standing MW, but PDU revenue follows new-build flow — which is concentrating outside CE-7
hypotheses.narrative: The CE-7 = 39.8%-of-Europe hop divides standing colocation stock by standing stock. But rack-PDU revenue is fit-out-driven: it is earned when MW are delivered or refreshed, not while they sit installed. CBRE shows the flow concentrating away from CE: 57% of 2025's 937MW of new European supply lands in FLAPD (of which only Frankfurt and Amsterdam are CE-7), 85% of take-up is FLAPD, London alone exceeds 1GW and is Europe's largest market, and double-digit growth in secondary markets is going to Milan and Madrid — not Warsaw or Vienna. If CE-7's share of European capacity ADDITIONS is meaningfully below its 39.8% share of stock, the €300M systematically overstates where 2026 PDU purchase orders will actually be written, even if every input number is individually correct.

mechanism.text: European colo revenue-generating activity is flow-shaped: 937MW of new supply in 2025, 57% of it in FLAPD, and 85% of take-up in FLAPD — a distribution measurably different from the standing-stock table the CE-7 ratio is computed from

London (>1GW, Europe's largest market) and Frankfurt together represent roughly half of European supply, and CBRE's tracked secondary-growth markets are Milan and Madrid — so the growth increment that a 9% CAGR is made of accrues disproportionately to non-CE-7 geographies, while CE-7's standing stock skews toward older, lower-density halls with slower PDU refresh

The 39.8% stock ratio overstates CE-7's share of near-term PDU revenue; tamBase should be read as a stock-weighted ceiling, with the flow-weighted (order-relevant) base materially lower — a bias no band edge in the tornado captures because it is a method error, not parameter noise

hypotheses.whyMissable: Stock apportionment is the standard top-down move and every input is sourced and recent — nothing on the page looks estimated. The rot is in an unstated physical assumption (PDU € per standing MW is uniform across regions and vintages) that no source states because no source measures it; you only see it by asking what event actually generates a PDU invoice.
hypotheses.falsifier: CBRE's 2025–26 delivery pipeline (or Statista's 2026-vintage country deltas) showing CE-7 capturing ≥38% of new European colocation MW delivered — i.e., flow share matching stock share.

likelihood.rationale: The FLAPD concentration of additions and take-up is well documented, but Frankfurt and Amsterdam are themselves FLAPD members inside CE-7, which claws back part of the flow; the net CE-7 flow deficit vs its stock share is real but plausibly 5–10 points rather than catastrophic

perturbation.note: Re-weight the CE-7 share from standing stock toward capacity-additions flow (~32% vs 39.8%)

indicators.signal: CE-7 share of new European colocation MW delivered per quarter
indicators.where: CBRE 'Europe Data Centres Figures' quarterly PDFs (new supply by market)
indicators.threshold: CE-7 markets (Frankfurt, Amsterdam, Zurich, Warsaw, Vienna, Prague) <35% of tracked deliveries for two consecutive quarters

indicators.signal: Growth differential between CE-7 and non-CE secondary markets
indicators.where: Statista Europe colocation power by country, 2026 vintage vs 2025; CBRE secondary-markets commentary (Milan, Madrid)
indicators.threshold: Non-CE secondary markets adding MW at ≥2x the CE-7 ex-DE/NL rate

mitigations.action: Rebuild the CE base on a two-component model: refresh revenue on standing stock plus fit-out revenue on the CBRE delivery pipeline, priced per MW from vendor BOMs
mitigations.note: Separates the stock-vs-flow question the current single ratio conflates

mitigations.action: Position Year-1 offering around retrofit/refresh of CE-7's older standing stock (where stock-based sizing IS the right measure) rather than new-build tenders
mitigations.note: Aligns go-to-market with the component of the TAM the model measures correctly

hypotheses.title: The €360M ceiling was set with the same contaminated-category trick it claimed to remove — the real base may sit above the band and the plan is sized for the wrong market
hypotheses.narrative: Refinement cycle 1 lowered the band top from €495M to €360M by comparing the BIS ceiling's 21% growth against a '10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR (IndexBox)'. But that 10–12% figure comes from IndexBox's €8.5–9.5B EU 'Power and Cable Management' report — a category several times broader than rack PDUs — so the correction that purged one contaminated number imported another. Meanwhile Germany alone is forecast at $509.6M in a PDU category by 2031, already exceeding the entire CE-7 base, and the global anchors span $1.7–3.94B, a 2.3x spread wider than the band. If the true CE base sits at €400M+, the failure mode is not embarrassment — it is a Year-1 plan whose certification scope, channel headcount, and manufacturing commitments were sized for €165M of SAM while incumbents scale into the €80–120M the model never showed the board.

mechanism.text: The band-top-lowering comparator ('10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR, IndexBox') is sourced from the EU Power and Cable Management report — a €8.5–9.5B multi-category basket — not a rack-PDU report, replicating the exact definitional bundling the refinement flagged in the BIS ceiling

Independent evidence points above the band: Germany's broader-PDU forecast alone reaches $509.6M by 2031, and same-category 2025 global anchors span $1.7B to $3.94B — a spread that exceeds the ±20% band and makes the €360M cap an artifact of one contested comparison rather than a bound

With tamBase capped at €360M, SAM and the Year-1 obtainable plan are calibrated low; under-sizing systematically under-provisions certification breadth (serviceable reach) and channel capacity in the launch window, which is unrecoverable in a market where gear is specified 12–24+ months ahead

hypotheses.whyMissable: Downward refinements read as rigor — 'we caught an inflated ceiling and cut it' is exactly what a diligent team wants on the record, so nobody audits the comparator that justified the cut. Upside error also feels safe ('conservative is fine'), hiding that under-sizing misallocates real resources: too-narrow cert scope and too-thin channel for the market that actually exists.
hypotheses.falsifier: The IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report headline (once purchased) landing at or below €360M for the 2025/2026 EU base, confirming the lowered ceiling despite the contaminated comparator.

likelihood.rationale: Multiple broader-category datapoints point above the band, but each carries the same definitional contamination in the upward direction; the honest read is that the ceiling is unproven rather than proven wrong

perturbation.note: Base above the current band top, consistent with the Germany-alone $509.6M signal and the upper global anchors

indicators.where: IndexBox EU Rack PDUs and Germany Rack PDUs reports ($4,000 each); Omdia rack-PDU vendor share table

indicators.signal: Incumbent EMEA rack-power growth outrunning the model's 9% CAGR
indicators.where: Vertiv and Legrand quarterly earnings calls, EMEA data-center product-line commentary
indicators.threshold: Two consecutive quarters of >15% YoY EMEA rack-power growth

mitigations.action: Commission the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report to fix the base directly — it resolves this hypothesis and the Europe-share hop simultaneously
mitigations.note: The ledger's own promotionPath; a $4,000 spend against a €60M+ band question

mitigations.action: Build the Year-1 operating plan with staged capacity options (certification scope and contract manufacturing volumes expandable at 6-month gates) rather than fixed to the €165M SAM
mitigations.note: Makes the plan robust to the base being above the band without pre-committing capital
